reviews.text: To me a really great example of originality and provocation meaning very little if a film doesn’t deliver on its presented ideas , it’s more interesting in structure than content but even the shifting POV doesn’t amount to much. There’s a stretch in the middle (the best part imo) between a disgruntled cop and a young troublemaker where Cregger seems to be introducing elements of coincidence, human fallibility, and even how our institutions can fail to protect our kids, but otherwise the whole Magnolia thing is mostly a gimmick to control when information is revealed. There are so many intriguing dynamics in this town that are ultimately dead-ended in service of a ~crazy~ third act that people will hoot and holler about, but despite more sophisticated filmmaking after Barbarian this feels even more fractured in its ambitions. Many more thoughts… didn’t hate it but didn’t get much out of it either.

reviews.text: crowd was dead silent for the entire film– except for when stephen mckinley henderson was on screen. everybody lost their damn minds. just absolutely constant screaming. throwing shit at the screen, taking flash photos. whole theater on beatlemania mode. large and loud boos the second he was off-screen, i couldn't even hear the dialogue. heard sniffling in the audience when stephen mckinley henderson's character offered his resignation, saw a father and son embracing in joy when oscar isaac turned it down. three rows started swag surfing when henderson's eyes rolled in the back of his head. when his name came up in the credits it was like we won the super bowl or some shit. i quitely asked my friends…

reviews.text: Realizing that Emerald Fennell so wildly misread this novel as a swoony teenager and that it then became a core artistic touchstone for her really unlocks her whole deal. Jettisoning the text's intrinsic racialism, classism, and story of abuse as love in favor of recasting Cathy and Heathcliff as doomed lovers locked in mutual acrimonious kink is certainly an eminently memeable take, and fealty to source is no barometer for success in adaptation, but this movie isn't even momentarily discomfiting to compensate.

reviews.author: timtamtitus

reviews.text: I’m not a purist about adaptations. Change things. Reinterpret. Go wild. But this version of Wuthering Heights feels less like an adaptation and more like someone skim read the blurb, vibesed for a bit, then decided Emily Brontë was simply being too intense and needed calming down. The big problem is tone. Or rather, the complete lack of the one Brontë actually wrote. This is a novel powered by spite, obsession, cruelty, class rage, and emotional violence. It is feral. This film is… soft. Glossy. Polite. It treats one of the angriest books ever written like a doomed romance you might half watch while scrolling. Heathcliff is the biggest casualty. Casting Jacob Elordi turns him into a sad eyed dreamboat…
